revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine]][[id:Kuil Heisenji Hakusan]][[ja:平泉寺白山神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Heisenji Hakusan Shrine
平泉寺白山神社 | image = File:Heisenji Hakusanjinjya HAIDEN.jpg | caption = Precincts of Heisenji Hakusan Shrine | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.043917|N|136.542131|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Heisenji Hakusan Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Izanami|qid=Q682306|1=Izanami}} | founded_by ={{ill|Taichō|qid=Q2388439|1=Taichō}} | established = 717 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 6 Heisenjichō Heisenji, Katsuyama-shi, Fukui-ken 911-0822 | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} | festival = April 20 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} | location = {{ill|Heisenji|qid=Q17176452|1=Heisenji}}; {{ill|Katsuyama|qid=Q847543|1=Katsuyama, Fukui}} }} {{nihongo|'''Heisenji Hakusan Shrine'''|平泉寺白山神社|Heisenji Hakusan jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Katsuyama, Fukui|en|Katsuyama, Fukui|qid=Q847543|lt=Katsuyama|1=Katsuyama, Fukui}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|qid=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}, Japan. In the former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], it was a prefectural shrine of Fukui Prefecture. Noted for its mossy gardens, the shrine gardens were designated a National Place of Scenic Beauty (名勝) in 1930 and a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1935.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138244 |title= 白山平泉寺旧境内|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}} Its main festival is held annually on April 20. The area of the shrine is within the boundaries of {{ill|Hakusan National Park|en|Hakusan National Park|qid=Q1570081|lt=Hakusan National Park|1=Hakusan National Park}}.{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} ==History== A {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}, Heisen-ji, was founded in 717 AD by the Japanese [[Shugendō]] monk {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|qid=Q2388439|lt=Taichō|1=Taichō}}, who is said to have been the first person to ascend the {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|qid=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountains|1=Sacred mountains}} {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|qid=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan|1=Mount Haku}} (2,702m)[http://haku-san.com/mod/history/index.php?content_id=13 Taichō Kashō Denki] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100304210320/http://haku-san.com/mod/history/index.php?content_id=13 |date=2010-03-04 }}. Katsuyama Kyōdoshi Kenkyūkai. Accessed November 17, 2010. It became a centre for {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|qid=Q1595289|lt=mountain worship|1=Sacred mountains}} involving amalgamation of beliefs, philosophies, doctrines and ritual systems drawn from local folk-religious practices, pre-Buddhist mountain worship, [[Shinto]], [[Taoism]] and {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}}{{cite book|last1=Kornicki|first1=P.F.|last2=McMullen|first2=I. J.|title=Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth|date=1996|publisher=Cambridge University Press|location=Cambridge|isbn=9780521550284|pages=13–|edition=Reprint|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gsLDwvmnt_oC&pg=PA13|accessdate=11 March 2017|language=en}} and ascetic practices involving climbing the mountain by various routes. The temple was mentioned in the ''[[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]]'', and was the site of the 1183 [[:en:Battle of Kurikara|Battle of Kurikara]] during the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|qid=Q737542|lt=Genpei War|1=Genpei War}} between [[:en:Kiso Yoshinaka|Kiso Yoshinaka]] and the [[Taira clan]]. The temple was destroyed in 1338 by the forces of {{ill|Shiba Takatsune|en|Shiba Takatsune|qid=Q7496005|lt=Shiba Takatsune|1=Shiba Takatsune}} for its support of the [[Southern Court]] of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|qid=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} and again in 1440. It was rebuilt with the support of the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]], which levied a special tax on the seven provinces of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|qid=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} for its reconstruction. As a result, the temple expanded to a powerful religious city consisting of 48 shrines and 36 temples, along with an urban complex of over 6000 living quarters for priests or shrine personnel. The temple precincts governed a domain yielding revenues of 90,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'', giving it a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|qid=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' which rivalled that of a ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'', and it could field an army of 8000 ''[[sōhei]]'' warrior-monks. However, the whole temple was destroyed again in 1574 by {{ill|Asakura Kageaki|en|Asakura Kageaki|qid=Q4803439|lt=Asakura Kageaki|1=Asakura Kageaki}} during the Echizen {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|qid=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}} uprising. Despite subsequent effort of reconstruction, the temple precincts were reduced to less than one tenth its former area, and its ''kokudaka'' was reduced to 330 ''koku''; many ruins of temples were buried under forests and fields. Due to the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Ordinance Distinguishing Shinto and Buddhism]] issued at the beginning of [[:en:Meiji Period|Meiji Period]], the temple abandoned Buddhism and became a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}. ==Gallery== Heisenji Hakusan Shrine.JPG|[[Honden]], built in 1795 Heisenji, Hakusan - 05 - 2016-04-26.jpg|Approach to the shrine Heisennji-hakusannjinnjya-michi100.jpg|Monument marking the approach as one of the "100 roads of Japan" Heisenji, Hakusan - 17 - 2016-04-26.jpg|[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|qid=Q10222682|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Heisenji}} *[http://www.fuku-e.com/lang/english/places_to_visit/temple-shrine.php Shrines and Temples of Fukui Prefecture] *[http://archaeology.jp/sites/2009/07heisenji.htm Archaeology Japan] *[http://www.city.katsuyama.fukui.jp/heisenji/ Katsuyama City official site] {{Authority control}} [[en:Heisenji Hakusan Shrine]] {{Commons category|Heisenji}} [[ja:平泉寺白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11483206}} [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Katsuyama, Fukui]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
